findings.ref: tests/guard-r10-port.tests.ps1 (TEST-001 corpus) vs requirements.md Risks / T-001 Scope
findings.note: The parity corpus samples the protected table instead of exercising every protected-suffix class as the spec mandates ('parity fixtures must exercise every protected suffix class, not a sample' - flagged Critical in requirements.md Risks; repeated in T-001 Scope and design.md Test Strategy #2). Write-denial fixtures cover only 7 groups. Untested classes: kill-switch.{js,sh,ps1}, check-evidence-bundle.{sh,ps1,py}, validate_path.py, settings.local.json, tests/eval|guard-parity|constant-parity, the four sdd-review-loop reviewer-agent .md files, task-review-loop SKILL.md, hooks.json/copilot-hooks.json, and the entire $ProtectedGatePluginJsonSuffixes branch (a second suffix table with its own distinct matching loop), which has zero fixtures. Because the .py/.js twin sources are not in the bundle, decision parity is the only mechanism that could detect a table-content divergence, and it can only detect divergence for entries the corpus exercises. AC-001 ('any path in the protected table') is therefore only sample-verified, leaving the exact 'silently narrows Windows enforcement' critical risk open for uncovered entries.

The ASCII/no-BOM check defaults to the staged human-copy artifact, not the live plugins/sdd-quality-loop/scripts path. For this port the manifest hash ties staged to live, so AC-015 holds now, but as a standing suite check it guards frozen evidence rather than the file Windows hosts actually execute; a later non-ASCII change to the live .ps1 would pass the suite unless GUARD_PS1 is explicitly set.

Sanitization redactions replaced security-relevant source fragments, so those portions could not be independently reviewed byte-for-byte; verification of that logic rests on the RED/GREEN behavioral corpus and the recorded SHA-256 rather than direct inspection.
